title: The Exception Hierarchy
tagline: Know the family tree before you catch anything

sections.heading: Why the hierarchy matters
sections.body:
Every exception in Java is an object, and every one of them descends from a single root class: Throwable. Understanding this tree is the difference between catching exceptions by reflex and catching them with intent.

The compiler treats different branches of this tree very differently. Some it forces you to handle. Others it lets you ignore. If you don't know which branch you're standing on, you can't reason about what the compiler will and won't demand from you.

sections.heading: The tree, from root to leaf
sections.body:
Throwable                       // the root of everything you can throw or catch
├── Error                       // serious JVM-level problems — do NOT catch these
│   ├── OutOfMemoryError
│   ├── StackOverflowError
│   └── VirtualMachineError
└── Exception                   // problems your program can reasonably handle
    ├── IOException             // CHECKED — compiler forces you to handle it
    ├── SQLException            // CHECKED
    ├── ClassNotFoundException  // CHECKED
    └── RuntimeException        // UNCHECKED — compiler does NOT force handling
        ├── NullPointerException
        ├── IllegalArgumentException
        ├── IllegalStateException
        ├── IndexOutOfBoundsException
        └── ArithmeticException

sections.heading: The three categories that matter
sections.body:
1. Error — Something went catastrophically wrong inside the JVM itself: out of memory, stack overflow, a corrupted class file. These are not meant to be caught or recovered from. If you see one, your process is usually done.

2. Checked exceptions — Direct subclasses of Exception (but NOT of RuntimeException). The compiler forces you to either catch them or declare them with throws. They represent recoverable conditions outside your control: a missing file, a dropped network connection, a locked database row.

3. Unchecked exceptions — Subclasses of RuntimeException. The compiler does not force you to handle them. They almost always represent programming bugs: dereferencing null, passing an invalid argument, indexing past the end of an array.

sections.heading: The one rule that defines everything
Throwable's useful methods

title: Checked Exceptions
tagline: The compiler makes you deal with these

sections.heading: What makes an exception checked
sections.body:
A checked exception is any subclass of Exception that is not a subclass of RuntimeException. The compiler enforces a contract: if a method can throw one, that fact must be visible in its signature, and every caller must consciously deal with it.

The intent is honesty. Checked exceptions represent failures that are outside the caller's control but entirely foreseeable — the file might be gone, the network might blink, the query might time out. Java forces the caller to acknowledge that reality at compile time rather than discover it in production.

sections.heading: The compiler's demand: handle or declare
Common checked exceptions in the JDK
Declaring throws up the call chain
The InterruptedException special case

title: Unchecked Exceptions
tagline: Usually a bug in your code, not a condition to recover from

sections.heading: What makes an exception unchecked
sections.body:
An unchecked exception is any subclass of RuntimeException (or Error). The compiler does not force you to catch or declare it. You can throw it from any method without touching the signature, and callers are free to ignore it.

This freedom exists for a reason: unchecked exceptions almost always signal a programming error — a broken assumption, a bug in the logic, a contract the caller violated. You cannot meaningfully recover from most of them because the correct fix is to change the code, not to handle the exception at runtime.

sections.heading: The everyday unchecked exceptions
Throwing unchecked exceptions to guard your code
Why you should almost never catch NullPointerException
Unchecked exceptions propagate silently

title: When to Use Each
tagline: The decision framework senior engineers actually use

sections.heading: The core question
sections.body:
For every failure you model, ask one question: Can the immediate caller reasonably be expected to recover from this?

If yes — the caller can retry, fall back, or take a meaningful alternative action — a checked exception makes that recovery a compile-time obligation. If no — the failure is a bug, or nothing can be done except report and abort — an unchecked exception keeps your code clean and lets the failure propagate to a central handler.

This is the rule the JLS and Effective Java both endorse: use checked exceptions for recoverable conditions, unchecked exceptions for programming errors.

sections.heading: The decision tree
sections.body:
Q1: Is this a bug — a broken precondition or invariant?
     YES → UNCHECKED (IllegalArgumentException, IllegalStateException, NPE)
     NO  → continue

Q2: Can the caller do something useful about it (retry, fall back, ask the user)?
     YES → CHECKED  (the compiler will make sure they consider it)
     NO  → UNCHECKED (let it bubble to a global handler)

Q3: Are you writing a modern framework-style API (like Spring)?
     Prefer UNCHECKED — modern APIs favour unchecked to keep signatures clean
     and to work smoothly with lambdas and streams.

// When genuinely unsure, most modern Java leans UNCHECKED.
// Checked exceptions are the deliberate exception, not the default.

sections.heading: Concrete examples, side by side
The modern trend: unchecked by default
sections.body:
The industry has drifted decisively toward unchecked exceptions over the last two decades, and it's worth understanding why.

- Checked exceptions don't compose with lambdas and streams. You cannot throw a checked exception from a Function or a Supplier without ugly wrapping, which makes them painful in functional-style code.

- Checked exceptions leak across abstraction layers. A SQLException thrown from your repository forces your service and controller to know about JDBC — a violation of encapsulation.

- They encourage bad handling. Under pressure, developers write empty catch blocks just to make the compiler happy, which is worse than no handling at all.

Spring embraced this fully: it wraps every checked SQLException in an unchecked DataAccessException. That's why you almost never write throws in a Spring service.

sections.heading: When checked exceptions still earn their place

title: Custom Exceptions
tagline: Model your domain's failures as first-class types

sections.heading: Why create your own exception types
sections.body:
Generic exceptions like RuntimeException or IllegalStateException tell you that something failed, but not what failed in business terms. Custom exceptions make failures self-documenting: UserNotFoundException, InsufficientFundsException, and OrderAlreadyShippedException read like the domain they model.

They also let callers catch precisely what they care about, and they give a global handler clean hooks to map each failure to the right HTTP status. A well-designed exception type is part of your API's contract.

sections.heading: A well-designed unchecked domain exception
Always provide constructors that accept a cause
Building an exception hierarchy for your app

tagline: The habits that separate senior from mid

Write messages that help the person debugging at 3am

title: Exceptions in Spring Boot
tagline: How real APIs turn exceptions into clean HTTP responses

sections.heading: Spring's philosophy: unchecked everywhere
sections.body:
Spring made a deliberate architectural choice: it converts checked exceptions into unchecked ones so your business code stays clean. The most visible example is data access — every low-level, checked SQLException is caught by Spring and re-thrown as an unchecked DataAccessException.

This is why a typical Spring service method has no throws clause and no try/catch for database errors. You let unchecked exceptions propagate and handle them in exactly one place: a global exception handler. This keeps controllers and services focused on business logic, not error plumbing.

sections.heading: The global handler: @RestControllerAdvice
Let domain exceptions drive HTTP status
@ResponseStatus — the lightweight shortcut
Validation exceptions and a consistent error body
